nativeRange.habitat: Bucephalandra 'Godzilla' is a hobby cultivar derived from the Bucephalandra genus, which is endemic to the island of Borneo. Wild Bucephalandra grow as rheophytes — plants that anchor to submerged rocks and wood in fast-moving water — along the banks and stream beds of Borneo's rainforest rivers.

decorPreferences: lava rock, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
open space around the attachment point so the large leaves can extend without being crowded
low-to-moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic rheophyte stream conditions
stable, established tank — generally advised against introducing into newly cycled systems

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm on the broad leaves
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Java fern, mosses and other Bucephalandra cultivars
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers such as large cichlids that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters where buce melt is more likely

summary: Bucephalandra 'Godzilla' is a hobby cultivar of the Borneo-endemic Bucephalandra genus, prized for its unusually large, deeply rippled leaves. Like all Bucephalandra, it grows attached to hardscape — the rhizome must never be buried. It is generally considered one of the largest cultivars in the trade and benefits from open space around its anchor point.
careNotes:
Bucephalandra 'Godzilla' is a hobby cultivar of the Bucephalandra genus, a group of aquatic plants in the family Araceae that originates exclusively from Borneo. Like all Bucephalandra, it is grown as an epiphyte — a plant that attaches to surfaces rather than rooting in substrate — and is cared for in much the same way as Anubias. The rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves grow) must always be kept above the substrate and anchored to a piece of driftwood, lava rock, or other porous hardscape; burying it causes the plant to rot.

Water temperature is generally advised in the 21–28 °C range, with pH around 6.0–7.5. Godzilla tolerates low to medium light and is widely regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly Bucephalandra cultivars. CO2 supplementation is optional but commonly reported to produce noticeably richer coloration and faster growth. Low-level water-column fertilization is generally recommended, and stronger lighting combined with added nutrients tends to enhance both the depth of color and the iridescent sheen the leaves can develop.

Godzilla is generally considered one of the largest Bucephalandra cultivars available in the hobby, with long, broad leaves that develop a distinctive ruffled or wavy edge. New leaves commonly emerge reddish to brown and gradually darken to green with maturity, sometimes displaying an iridescent sheen and pinkish-orange stems.

Like other Bucephalandra, this cultivar is prone to 'buce melt' — a temporary die-back of existing leaves — when first transitioning from the emersed (above-water) conditions of nursery growth to a fully submerged aquarium, or following a sudden change in water parameters. Recovery from a healthy, firm rhizome is generally reported over the following weeks as new growth resumes.
breedingNotes: Propagation is by rhizome division: cut the rhizome at a natural bend using clean scissors, leaving each piece with several leaves and visible roots, then reattach each division to a separate piece of hardscape using super glue gel, sewing thread, or fishing line. Godzilla is also reported to produce lateral side shoots from the rhizome, which can be detached once they have developed their own roots and a few leaves. Growth is slow, so a freshly divided plant typically needs several weeks before it begins putting out new growth.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirms origin (fast-flowing rivers of Borneo), temperature range 70-82°F (≈21-28 °C), low-to-moderate lighting, slow growth, midground-to-background placement, propagation by rhizome division and side shoots, requirement to attach to hardscape and never bury the rhizome, and characteristic bold, wavy leaves with deep green to bluish-purple coloration.

additional.name: Flip Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source (substituted after the intended Buceplant URL returned 404). Cross-checked: Borneo origin, beginner care level, low-to-medium lighting (high light enhances coloration), optional CO2, mid-to-background placement, propagation by rhizome cutting, attachment to driftwood/rock, and the cultivar-specific traits of longer leaves, ruffling, and brown/red submersed leaf coloration.

additional.name: Glass Aqua
additional.notes: Additional secondary used for sizing. Reports plant height of 3-5"+ (≈8-13 cm) with long, ruffled-edged leaves and dark green foliage; confirms easy care, low-to-medium light, slow growth, midground placement, optional CO2, propagation by division and attachment to hardscape, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, and susceptibility to algae on the slow-growing leaves.

additional.name: Bucephalandra (genus parent entry fw-plant-016)
additional.notes: Cross-referenced shared genus husbandry (water parameters 21-28 °C / pH 6-8, low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, slow growth, epiphytic placement, do-not-bury-rhizome rule, buce melt during emersed-to-submersed transition, propagation by rhizome division). The genus entry already documents Godzilla as one of the taller cultivars in the 15-25 cm range; this cultivar entry inherits that genus baseline.
